headlines.title: JAPAN PUBLIC APPROVES WAR; SEES VICTORY
headlines.subtitle: Newspapers Urge That Courtesy Be Shown to Germans.
headlines.fullText: TOKIO, Aug. 24, 12:15 a. m .- Unan- the war summarizes public opinion. The newspaers re- Pre the necessity of hostilities with Germany and urge the public to re- train from exhibiting resentment to- ward Germans in Japan. They print messages of cheer to the army and navy. An imperial messen- ger went to the shrines of Ine to inform the spirits of the imperial anon iman- Japan's declaration war, nounced that a flock of white doves de. ncended in the roof of the imperial tum of Germany expired and this is accepted as an omen of speedy vic- The ladies' patriotic association. which has a membership of 100,000, is sending to the volunteer nurses pack- ages for the soldiers and will provide for the familles of those who have been called to the service. The Red Cross Society Is equipping a hospital ship.

headlines.title: GREAT GERMAN FORCE MOVING AGAINSTFRANCE
headlines.subtitle: More Than 200,000 Passed Through City in 48 Hours, Says Englishman.
headlines.fullText: LONDON, Aug. 24 .-- A dispatch to the Daily Telegraph from Ostend says: An Englishman who succeeded to getting out of Brussels today (prob- ably Sunday) makes statement regarding conditions there: "German troops are being rushed southward through Brussels as rapidly "The general estimate was that 200,- 600 had passed through during the city. German officers told me their companies were making from thirty to forty miles a day. "The equipment of all the German forces is wonderful. Every detall Is perfect. I noticed that of the thousands of carts which passed, not one had been requisitioned but all were built for mil- frary service and bore the government with an extra set of shoes, which hang from the stirrups. "At Waterloon I saw a part of their ward Mons. I counted 230 guna. "The Bourse and general postoffice In Brussels for have been turned into dor- troops."

headlines.title: BODIES OF TWO RIVER VICTIMS ARE RECOVERED
headlines.subtitle: Dynamite Believed Have Loosened Them From Mud. Many Join in Search.
headlines.fullText: Brought to surface by blasts of dynamite, the the bodies of Oscar Hamm. 20, and HI. W. Kendrick, 32, the two North Side men who were drowned in Worth dam last Friday, were Teco- ered Sunday afternoon far below the pota, where they went undertrang by the swift current, and lodged In "pockets" of the river. The dynamite charges, which were touched off by ened the bodies from the mud of the river and brought them up. The body of Kendrick was seen float- ing at a point one mile and a half be- low the dam by Tom Castleberry, an old North Side Test nhanh Five men in a pienie party nearby took off and their recovered clothes, the swam into the stream body. Hamm's body was found a half mile farther down stream, at a point near the bridge mit in the stream and wam after it. The body floated against the overhanging limb of a tree and stuck there until Gillespie reached it. With the assistance of other men, the body was brought to angle

headlines.title: BELGIANS ATTACKED FOR ALLEGED CRUELTY
headlines.fullText: LONDON, Aug. 24 .- The Daily Mail's Rotterdam correspondent quotes the following from the Cologne Gazette In Justification of the fines levied on Brussels and Liege: "The war tribute is a punishment for the bad treatment of Germans by Belgians two weeks ago. When innocent To the tender mercies of the mob. -what happened there will ever sagen the names at the mellan people. We deliberated as to what would be a fitting requital and It seems the punisher, Pelas tim, is by legal process. A war tax allowed by the law of nations is only just. She must suffer all the burdens allowed by the legal right of law, such as quat- tering troops and furnishing supplies. until an her resources are exhausted. monally that hunting defenseless women In in different from having an enemy in their own homen. "The legal punishmene for offenses of the Belgians will be im- posed as fully as allowed by law."

headlines.title: Jack, the Giant Killer, Shoots 'Em by the Score
headlines.fullText: By Associated Press. LONDON, Aug. 24 .- Lieutenant Hen- kart, who is attached to the general staff of the Belgian army, is likely to prove a popular hero in the war. according to an Antwerp dispatch to the Daily Chronicle. He is said to have perfected a monitor armored motor car which has been successfully used by the Belgiana. Reconnoitering in one of his armored cars, Lieutenant Henkart has had sev- eral encounters With commande ontem His associates are a chatfeur, an en- "On Aug. 15 the party killed five Uh- tane: on Auf. 17 they killed seven and on Aug. 20, near Waterloo, they faced a force of nearly 500 Germans and en- caped, after killing twenty-five with a rapid-tirer which was mounted in the met motor a force car. of thirty On Aug. 21 the party Uhlan scouts and killed twenty.

headlines.title: 250 GERMAN COLUMNS NOW NEARING LILLE
headlines.fullText: LONDON. Aug. 24. 5:24 a. m-The Ostend correspondent of the Daily Mail says the German columns are march- ing southward toward Valenciennes in the Seheldt, thirty-one miles southeast of Lille. One column in proceeding by seiner, Belgium, and the other is going by way of Hal, Braine-le-Comte and Mons. Belkihm. Further northwest advance merely parties det an Lichtervelde. It Is suggest. ed that this means a dash upon Rou- baix, one of the wealthiest of French towns, or upon Lille.
